---
title: "Welcome to the Agentic AI Course!"
---

# Course Objectives

- Design, diagram, implement, evaluate, productize, and scale agentic AI systems using modern LLM frameworks and tooling.
- Identify and evaluate problems that are appropriate for agentic AI solutions, and distinguish them from problems better solved by traditional software or non-agentic LLM approaches.
- Clearly define Agentic AI and related concepts, including agents, tools, planning, grounding, orchestration, evaluation, and human-in-the-loop workflows.
- Develop and articulate ethical guidelines for the use of LLMs and agentic AI, including responsible data usage, AI governance principles, safety, privacy, and organizational policy considerations.
- Build the capability to independently learn, evaluate, and adapt to new AI models, tools, and paradigms in a rapidly evolving AI ecosystem.

---

# Course Overview

## Unit 1: Build a "Bad" Agent (Weeks 1–4)

  - [Unit 1](units/unit1.html)

**Goal:** Learn the tools to create a powerful but uncontrollable agent.

**Topics:**
- Intro to agent concepts and LLM programming
- API keys, environment setup, and class materials
- Making API calls, defining and calling tools, and managing statefulness
- Connecting tools: MCP, file system, browser use, internet search, custom functions
- Sandboxing with LangChain / Daytona
- Subagents and multi-agent architectures
- Writing effective prompts from scratch

**Project:** Personal Assistant Agent

Build a personal assistant agent that connects to real tools — Canvas, Google Calendar, Slack, the file system, and the web — and carries on a coherent, stateful conversation. You will write your own prompts from scratch; no AI, peer, or internet-generated prompts are permitted. The agent will be stress-tested in class to deliberately surface its weaknesses, and those failures become the raw material for Unit 2.

**Outcomes:**
- Call an LLM and receive a response
- Write original prompts for agent context
- Define custom tools and turn existing code into tools
- Connect and invoke tools (MCP, file system, browser, web search, Canvas, Google Calendar, Slack)
- Implement conversation history manually and with built-in methods
- Introduce statefulness to an agent
- Run agent code safely in a sandboxed environment
- Build a simple two-agent pipeline
- Demonstrate a working personal assistant agent and document where it breaks

**Note on AI use:** AI assistance is not permitted this unit. Debugging help comes from class time and the Slack group (ChatClass). This is intentional — it builds class unity, surfaces your own assumptions, and gives you room to fail. Failure is a core part of this unit, not a grading penalty. Set a time limit on any problem; if you're stuck, post in Slack or bring it to class unfinished. That is OK.

**Daily Schedule:**

| Day | Topic |
|-----|-------|
| 1 | Orientation — meet the professor, course objectives, demo of what you'll build, requirements and expectations, hype Battle of the Bots |
| 2 | APIs & Your First LLM Call — define an API, make a call, handle common challenges |
| 3 | Tools: Building & Using Them — custom tools, the @ decorator, LangChain Community tools |
| 4 | Statefulness & Conversation History — manual and built-in conversation history |
| 5 | Data & Local Resources — local data, file system access, running code |
| 6 | Connecting to the World — web search, browser use, MCP connections |
| 7 | Sandboxing & Safety — LangChain/Daytona sandboxing, isolated code execution |
| 8 | Subagents & Multi-Agent Intro — when to delegate, simple two-agent pipeline |
| 9 | Prompting Without AI — writing effective system prompts from scratch, iterating on output |
| 10 | Project Kickoff — design the personal assistant, plan tools and personality |
| 11 | Build Day — assemble tools into a working agent |
| 12 | Showcase & Stress-Test — demo agents, deliberately break them, document failures |

---

## Unit 2: Diagnostics — Why Is It "Bad"? (Weeks 5–8)

  - [Unit 2](units/unit2.html)

**Goal:** Develop diagnostic tools to peer into the black box of agentic AI, in preparation for building reliable agents.

**Topics:**
- Categories of agent failure: errors, loops, cost explosion, hallucination, unreliability
- Observability and tracing with LangSmith
- Token and latency analysis
- Debugging hallucinations and unexpected behavior
- Prompt failure patterns
- Cost and loop failure analysis
- Identifying and fixing code bugs

**Project:** Diagnostic Report — Fix a Broken Agent

You are given a pre-built broken agent to diagnose. Your job is to implement tracing, identify every failure, document root causes, and produce a structured diagnostic report. You will implement at least one fix and verify it with traces before presenting to the class.

**Outcomes:**
- Set up LangSmith and capture screenshot traces
- Implement tracing features and explain what each one does and why
- Measure token usage and latency across tool calls and LLM responses
- Identify categories of agent failure from trace data
- Distinguish model errors from prompt errors from code bugs
- Identify and rewrite failing prompts based on traced results
- Implement basic loop guards and context limits
- Fix identified bugs and verify with re-tracing
- Produce and present a diagnostic report

**Note on AI use:** AI assistance may be permitted this unit — details TBD. If used, post in Slack what you discussed and any lessons learned.

**Daily Schedule:**

| Day | Topic |
|-----|-------|
| 1 | What Makes an Agent "Bad"? — review Unit 1 failures, define failure categories, introduce the diagnostic mindset |
| 2 | Observability & Tracing — LangSmith setup, API key, navigating the interface |
| 3 | Scavenger Hunt — find specific traces and failure signatures in LangSmith |
| 4 | Token & Latency Analysis — understand cost growth, measure slowness and expense |
| 5 | Debugging Hallucinations & Unexpected Behavior — trace errors back to their source |
| 6 | Finding & Fixing Code Bugs — systematic debugging workflow, fix and verify |
| 7 | Prompt Failure Patterns — identify common prompt failure modes, rewrite and compare |
| 8 | Cost & Loop Failure Deep Dive — trace loops, implement loop guards and context limits |
| 9 | Comparative Diagnosis — contrast two agents on the same task, synthesize design principles |
| 10 | Analyze a Broken Agent — run, trace, and document every failure |
| 11 | Build the Diagnostic Report — categorize failures, document root causes, implement a fix |
| 12 | Present & Discuss — share reports, find class-wide patterns, bridge to context engineering |

---

## Unit 3: Context Engineering — Make a "Good" Agent (Weeks 9–12)

  - [Unit 3](units/unit3.html)

**Goal:** Engineer context effectively to produce agents that are reliable, specialized, and capable of operating over long horizons — then prove it in competition.

**Topics:**
- Context engineering vs. prompt engineering
- The 4 levels of intent: session, user, organization, world
- Simple context management: truncation, summarization, selection, file write, database, isolation/delegation
- Context offloading: semantic search, forgetting vector, graph memory
- Subagent architecture: parallelization, serialization, concurrency
- Checkpointing, human-in-the-loop, and context self-adaptation
- Grounding and RAG
- Long-horizon task planning
- Cost-aware context design

**Project:** Battle of the Bots

Apply everything from all three units to build a production-ready agent of your own design. Agents compete in structured challenges testing usefulness, resilience, and cost efficiency. The best agent is not necessarily the most powerful — it is the most reliable, the most purposeful, and the most improved from where you started in Unit 1.

**Outcomes:**
- Define context engineering and distinguish it from prompt engineering
- Implement truncation and summarization-based context management
- Apply context offloading using semantic search or graph memory
- Implement parallelized and serialized subagent patterns
- Add checkpointing and human-in-the-loop interrupts
- Build a RAG pipeline and test retrieval quality
- Design agents that maintain state across long multi-step tasks
- Apply context engineering improvements to the Unit 1 personal assistant
- Compete in Battle of the Bots and articulate design decisions made

**Daily Schedule:**

| Day | Topic |
|-----|-------|
| 1 | What Is Context Engineering? — definition, levels of intent, why prompting alone isn't enough |
| 2 | Simple Context Management — truncate, summarize, select, offload; implement truncation and summarization |
| 3 | Context Offloading — semantic search, forgetting vector, graph approaches |
| 4 | Subagent Architecture — parallelization, serialization, Ralph Wiggum Loop, deep research agent |
| 5 | Checkpointing, HITL & File Systems — messy file navigation, checkpoints, human-in-the-loop |
| 6 | Context Self-Adaptation & Cost Saving — adaptive context, cost reduction, full toolkit review |
| 7 | Grounding & RAG — connect to a real data source, build a RAG pipeline, tune retrieval |
| 8 | Long-Horizon Task Planning — to-do list management, multi-step state, complex task execution |
| 9 | Putting It All Together — revisit the Unit 1 agent, apply context strategies, document improvements |
| 10 | Final Agent Design — choose tools, context strategy, memory approach, and subagent structure |
| 11 | Final Build Day — full build session, peer testing and stress-testing |
| 12 | Battle of the Bots! — structured challenges, audience voting, reflect on the full arc |
